summary: O'Neil critiques the Koch brothers' sophisticated information control strategy, where they've shifted from openly libertarian rhetoric to philanthropic 'window dressing' while continuing harmful practices. She connects this to Detroit's water crisis, where 15,000 residents had water shut off to facilitate privatization. The post warns that private 'solutions' to public crises allow wealthy individuals to accumulate control over essential infrastructure while being celebrated as heroes. O'Neil argues this represents a dangerous asymptotic limit where intermittent crises become opportunities for the rich to consolidate power over public resources.
key_points: The Koch brothers changed their public rhetoric to pro-environment messaging while continuing to face pollution violations, demonstrating how wealth enables information control
Out-of-court settlements for corporate misdeeds prevent investigative journalism and public accountability—we only learn details when cases become public record
Detroit's water shutoffs affect 15,000 residents owing an average of $540, done to privatize the water system with favorable Wall Street terms
Private philanthropy solving public crises creates a dangerous dynamic where the wealthy accumulate infrastructure ownership while being celebrated as saviors
The UN declared Detroit's water shutoffs a human rights violation, yet no federal emergency was declared—suggesting poor victims are treated differently
money_quotes: It's not that they stopped polluting, it's that their rhetoric changed.
There's no way we can keep a score sheet with 'good deeds' on one side and 'shitty deeds' on the other. We don't have enough information for the second side.
The United Nations has called this water shutoff a violation of the human rights of the people of Detroit.
Wouldn't we be doing that in another city that had 15,000 residents without water? Why is this an exception to that rule? Because the victims are poor?

You can almost imagine David Koch coming to the rescue here, as part of his 'positive optics' campaign, and bailing out the Detroit citizens and then, for good measure, buying up the water system altogether. A hero!

A few very rich people gradually take control of resources, and then there are intermittent famines of various types in different cities, and the rich people swoop in and heroically save the day whilst scooping up even more ownership of what used to be public infrastructure.
It's frustrating to live in a country that has so many resources but which can't seem to get it together to meet the basic human needs of its citizens.
key_insight: When private philanthropy replaces public responsibility for basic needs like water, it creates a cycle where crises become opportunities for the wealthy to accumulate power while being celebrated as saviors.
